full_text:
Scorching Heat Grips Haryana and Punjab
Haryana and Punjab experienced blistering heat with temperatures soaring to abnormal highs. Rohtak in Haryana and Faridkot in Punjab recorded some of the highest temperatures, reaching above 44 degrees Celsius. The unrelenting heatwave engulfed other notable cities, affecting daily life and prompting health advisories.
In an intense heatwave, Haryana and Punjab sweltered on Saturday as temperatures surged well above normal. Cities like Rohtak and Faridkot led the spike, registering highs of 44.6 and 45.2 degrees Celsius, respectively, according to meteorological reports.
The relentless heat affected major towns across both states. In Haryana, Ambala reached a striking 41.9 degrees, Hisar 43 degrees, and Narnaul 43.5 degrees, among others. In Punjab, Amritsar and Ludhiana weren't spared, recording highs of 40.3 and 42.2 degrees Celsius.
Chandigarh, serving as a joint capital, also felt the severe heat, with temperatures peaking at 41 degrees Celsius. As the region continues to endure the weather extremes, authorities have urged residents to stay hydrated and avoid unnecessary sun exposure.
(With inputs from agencies.)

title: Heat wave to abate after 2 days, rain expected in parts of Punjab and Haryana from April 27
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds") Haryana
full_text:
Heat wave to abate after 2 days, rain expected in parts of Punjab and Haryana from April 27-May 1
Temperatures over the western Himalayan region are also expected to gradually fall by 2-3 degrees Celsius during April 28 to May 1
The ongoing spell of heat wave prevailing over parts of north-west India, where the mercury has crossed 44 degrees Celsius, is expected to abate after two days, with the weather department predicting isolated rain over parts of Punjab and Haryana from April 27 to May 1.
While heat wave conditions are expected to continue till April 27, light rain accompanied by thunderstorms, lightning and gusty winds are likely in both states subsequently, according to a bulletin issued by the India Meteorological Department (IMD) on April 25. No large change in maximum temperature is expected during the next three days, with a fall by about 2-4 degrees Celsius thereafter.
During the past 24 hours, the highest maximum temperature recorded in Haryana was 44.6 degrees Celsius at Rohtak, the highest maximum temperature in Punjab was 44.3 degrees Celsius at Bathinda.
Warm night conditions were also reported at several places, including Ambala, Narnaul, Gurugram and Rohtak in Haryana and Amritsar and Ludhiana in Punjab. IMD has termed the day and night temperatures to be appreciably above normal.
Though the weather in both states remained dry over the past few days, seasonal rains are above the long period average by 31 percent in Haryana and 25 percent in Punjab. From March 1 till April 25, Haryana received 30.2 mm rain against the normal of 23 mm for this period, while Punjab received 43.5 mm against the normal of 34.9 mm.
According to IMD a western disturbance in middle tropospheric westerlies with its axis at 5.8 km above mean sea level is prevailing over Central Asia, and a trough runs from northern parts of Uttar Pradesh to south-west Rajasthan adjoining Pakistan in lower tropospheric levels.
Besides light rain in Punjab and Haryana, these weather systems are likely to bring scattered to fairly widespread light to moderate rainfall or snowfall with thunderstorm, lightning and gusty winds over Jammu and Kashmir, Ladakh during April 25-28, and over Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand during April 28-30. Temperatures over the western Himalayan region are also expected to gradually fall by 2-3 degrees Celsius during April 28 to May 1.
Meanwhile, in a separate statement, the Ministry of Earth Sciences has stated that as per the latest assessment, maximum temperatures in many parts of northwest, central and peninsular India are currently ranging between 40-44 degrees Celsius.
In several regions, temperatures have been observed to be markedly above normal by 5 degrees Celsius or more, indicating a developing heat stress scenario across parts of the country.
In view of the prevailing and forecasted conditions, IMD has issued comprehensive heat wave safety guidelines and advised citizens to take necessary precautions. These include avoiding prolonged exposure to direct sunlight, especially during peak afternoon hours, staying adequately hydrated, wearing light and breathable clothing, and avoiding strenuous outdoor activities during high-temperature periods. Special care has been advised for vulnerable groups such as children, the elderly and individuals with pre-existing health conditions.
